section.title: The Song of Moses
section.sectionRationale: This is covenant lawsuit poetry: heaven and earth are called as witnesses while Moses indicts Israel's coming unfaithfulness to the Rock who bore, fed, and carried them, warns of the LORD's just anger, and yet closes with the LORD's own vindication of His people and His land, before Moses is told to climb Mount Nebo and view, but not enter, the land.
section.thesis: In this Song, God is praised as the Rock, utterly just and faithful, against whom Israel's coming rebellion, foreign gods, forgetfulness, and self-sufficient pride, will be measured; yet the LORD who disciplines His people will also vindicate them and repay their enemies, for vengeance belongs to Him alone, and the chapter closes with Moses himself sentenced to view, but never enter, the land because of his own failure at Meribah.
section.frameNote: Eight movements: heaven and earth called to witness as God is praised as the Rock, perfect and just; the LORD's tender care for Israel in the wilderness recalled; Jeshurun grown fat and forsaking the Rock of his salvation; the LORD's anger and hidden face in judgment; Israel's folly contrasted with the enemies' worthless rock; the LORD's reserved vengeance and His declaration, 'I am He'; the closing call to rejoice and take these words to heart, for they are life; and Moses commanded to climb Mount Nebo and view the land he will not enter.

units.label: Ascribe greatness to our God: the Rock
units.note: heaven and earth called to witness

verses.text: Give ear, O heavens, and I will speak; hear, O earth, the words of my mouth.
Let my teaching fall like rain and my speech settle like dew, like gentle rain on new grass, like showers on tender plants.
For I will proclaim the name of the LORD. Ascribe greatness to our God!
He is the {{tsur|Rock}}, His work is perfect; all His ways are just. A God of faithfulness without injustice, righteous and upright is He.
His people have acted corruptly toward Him; the spot on them is not that of His children, but of a perverse and crooked generation.
Is this how you repay the LORD, O foolish and senseless people? Is He not your Father and Creator? Has He not made you and established you?

units.label: Remember the days of old
units.note: the LORD's care in the wilderness

verses.text: Remember the days of old; consider the years long past. Ask your father, and he will tell you, your elders, and they will inform you.
When the Most High gave the nations their inheritance, when He divided the sons of man, He set the boundaries of the peoples according to the number of the sons of God.
But the LORD's portion is His people, Jacob His allotted inheritance.
He found him in a desert land, in a barren, howling wilderness; He surrounded him, He instructed him, He guarded him as the apple of His eye.
As an eagle stirs up its nest and hovers over its young, He spread His wings to catch them and carried them on His pinions.
The LORD alone led him, and no foreign god was with him.
He made him ride on the heights of the land and fed him the produce of the field. He nourished him with honey from the rock and oil from the flinty crag,
with curds from the herd and milk from the flock, with the fat of lambs, with rams from Bashan, and goats, with the choicest grains of wheat. From the juice of the finest grapes you drank the wine.

units.label: Jeshurun grew fat and forsook the Rock

verses.text: But {{Jeshurun|Jeshurun}} grew fat and kicked, becoming fat, bloated, and gorged. He abandoned the God who made him and scorned the Rock of his salvation.
They provoked His jealousy with foreign gods; they enraged Him with abominations.
They sacrificed to demons, not to God, to gods they had not known, to newly arrived gods, which your fathers did not fear.
You ignored the Rock who brought you forth; you forgot the God who gave you birth.

units.label: The LORD hides His face in judgment
units.note: a perverse generation, provoked to anger

verses.text: When the LORD saw this, He rejected them, provoked to anger by His sons and daughters.
He said: "I will hide My face from them; I will see what will be their end. For they are a perverse generation, children of unfaithfulness.

They have provoked My jealousy by that which is not God; they have enraged Me with their worthless idols. So I will make them jealous by those who are not a people; I will make them angry by a nation without understanding.
For a fire has been kindled by My anger, and it burns to the depths of Sheol; it consumes the earth and its produce, and scorches the foundations of the mountains.
I will heap disasters upon them; I will spend My arrows against them.
They will be wasted from hunger and ravaged by pestilence and bitter plague; I will send the fangs of wild beasts against them, with the venom of vipers that slither in the dust.
Outside, the sword will take their children, and inside, terror will strike the young man and the young woman, the infant and the gray-haired man.

units.label: Israel's folly, and the enemies' worthless rock
units.note: their rock is not like our Rock

verses.text: I would have said that I would cut them to pieces and blot out their memory from mankind,
if I had not dreaded the taunt of the enemy, lest their adversaries misunderstand and say: 'Our own hand has prevailed; it was not the LORD who did all this.'"
Israel is a nation devoid of counsel, with no understanding among them.
If only they were wise, they would understand it; they would comprehend their fate.
How could one man pursue a thousand, or two put ten thousand to flight, unless their Rock had sold them, unless the LORD had given them up?
For their rock is not like our Rock, even our enemies concede.
But their vine is from the vine of Sodom and from the fields of Gomorrah. Their grapes are poisonous; their clusters are bitter.
Their wine is the venom of serpents, the deadly poison of cobras.

units.label: Vengeance is Mine; I am He
units.note: the LORD's reserved justice and compassion

verses.text: "Have I not stored up these things, sealed up within My vaults?
{{naqam|Vengeance}} is Mine; I will repay. In due time their foot will slip; for their day of disaster is near, and their doom is coming quickly."
For the LORD will vindicate His people and have compassion on His servants when He sees that their strength is gone and no one remains, slave or free.
He will say: "Where are their gods, the rock in which they took refuge,
which ate the fat of their sacrifices and drank the wine of their drink offerings? Let them rise up and help you; let them give you shelter!
See now that I am He; there is no God besides Me. I bring death and I give life; I wound and I heal, and there is no one who can deliver from My hand.
For I lift up My hand to heaven and declare: As surely as I live forever,
when I sharpen My flashing sword, and My hand grasps it in judgment, I will take vengeance on My adversaries and repay those who hate Me.
I will make My arrows drunk with blood, while My sword devours flesh, the blood of the slain and captives, the heads of the enemy leaders."

units.label: Rejoice, O nations; take these words to heart

verses.text: Rejoice, O heavens, with Him, and let all God's angels worship Him. Rejoice, O nations, with His people; for He will avenge the blood of His children. He will take vengeance on His adversaries and repay those who hate Him; He will cleanse His land and His people.
Then Moses came with Joshua son of Nun and recited all the words of this song in the hearing of the people.
When Moses had finished reciting all these words to all Israel,
he said to them, "Take to heart all these words I testify among you today, so that you may command your children to carefully follow all the words of this law.
For they are not idle words to you, because they are your life, and by them you will live long in the land that you are crossing the Jordan to possess."

units.label: Moses is told to climb Mount Nebo and die

verses.text: On that same day the LORD said to Moses,
"Go up into the Abarim Range to Mount Nebo, in the land of Moab across from Jericho, and view the land of Canaan, which I am giving to the Israelites as their own possession.
And there on the mountain that you climb, you will die and be gathered to your people, just as your brother Aaron died on Mount Hor and was gathered to his people.
For at the waters of Meribah-kadesh in the Wilderness of Zin, both of you broke faith with Me among the Israelites by failing to treat Me as holy in their presence.
Although you shall see from a distance the land that I am giving the Israelites, you shall not enter it."

tsur.gloss: Rock. The central title for God in this Song, evoking unshakeable stability, refuge, and faithfulness. Israel's folly is trading this Rock for false, worthless rocks that cannot save, and Paul identifies the Rock that followed Israel in the wilderness as Christ Himself, 1 Corinthians 10:4.

Jeshurun.gloss: Jeshurun, an affectionate poetic name for Israel meaning something like 'upright one,' used here with heavy irony as the 'upright one' grows fat, rebellious, and crooked.

naqam.gloss: Vengeance, requital. 'Vengeance is Mine; I will repay' reserves the right of ultimate justice to God alone, quoted directly by Paul in Romans 12:19 to forbid personal revenge among believers.

basic.context: Moses recites this Song before all Israel as his final major address, covenant lawsuit poetry that calls heaven and earth as witnesses while indicting Israel's coming unfaithfulness to the Rock who formed, fed, and carried them through the wilderness. It moves through God's justice, Israel's coming rebellion and its consequences, the LORD's ultimate vindication of His people and vengeance on their enemies, and closes with the sober fact that Moses himself, sentenced for his own failure at Meribah, will only view the land from Mount Nebo before he dies.
basic.hermeneutics: This is genuinely poetic covenant lawsuit literature, using the ancient Near Eastern legal form of calling witnesses, heaven and earth, to a covenant dispute; its central image, God as Rock, unshakeable, faithful, and just, runs through the whole song and becomes directly christological when Paul identifies 'the Rock' that followed Israel in the wilderness as Christ Himself, 1 Corinthians 10:4.
basic.originalLanguages: God is repeatedly called tsur, Rock, stable, faithful, and the source of Israel's very life, contrasted with the false 'rock' of the nations that cannot save. Israel is addressed by the poetic name Jeshurun, the upright one, used ironically as they grow fat and rebellious. And the LORD reserves naqam, vengeance, entirely to Himself, forbidding His people from taking it into their own hands.
basic.typology: Paul explicitly identifies the Rock that gave Israel water in the wilderness as Christ Himself, 1 Corinthians 10:4, meaning this Song's central image of the Rock who fed, carried, and sustained Israel points directly to the pre-incarnate Christ sustaining His people even then.
basic.christ: 1 Corinthians 10:1-4 says Israel 'drank from the spiritual rock that followed them, and that rock was Christ,' identifying the very Rock this Song celebrates as the eternal Son who has always been present with and sustaining God's people, long before His incarnation.
basic.god: God is praised here as utterly just, 'all His ways are just... righteous and upright is He,' even while His discipline of His unfaithful people is severe; yet He reserves final vengeance for Himself alone and ultimately vindicates and has compassion on His people, showing His justice and His mercy are never in conflict.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 10:1-4 identifies the Rock of this Song as Christ. Romans 12:19 quotes 'Vengeance is Mine; I will repay' to forbid personal revenge among believers. Hebrews 10:30 quotes the same verse regarding God's judgment. Revelation 15:3 echoes the Song of Moses in the song of the redeemed before God's throne.
basic.oneStory: This covenant lawsuit poem indicts Israel's coming unfaithfulness to the Rock who sustained them, reserves vengeance for God alone, and anticipates His ultimate vindication of His people, themes that find their full resolution in Christ, the Rock who followed Israel and who bears the vengeance His people deserved so that mercy might triumph.

context.literary: This Song is the poetic centerpiece of the closing chapters of Deuteronomy, following its commissioning in chapter 31 and preceding Moses' final blessing of the tribes in chapter 33 and his death in chapter 34.
context.historical: Covenant lawsuit poetry, calling on witnesses like heaven and earth to a covenant dispute, was a known ancient Near Eastern legal and prophetic form; this Song functions almost as a pre-written verdict, given generations before Israel's actual apostasy and exile would fulfill it.
context.canonical: Revelation 15:3 shows the redeemed in heaven singing 'the song of Moses, the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb,' directly linking this ancient Song to the final praise of the redeemed.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To provide Israel, in the durable form of poetry meant to be memorized and sung, an enduring witness to God's faithfulness as the Rock, a warning of the real consequences of covenant unfaithfulness, and assurance of His ultimate vindication of His people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The Song's specific historical predictions concerning Israel's future apostasy and exile are prophetic description of Israel's particular covenant history; its central theological affirmations, God as the just and faithful Rock, vengeance reserved to God alone, and His ultimate compassion on His people, are directly prescriptive truths for God's people in every era.

debates.question: How should the 'sons of God' in verse 8 be understood?

views.label: Referring to divine or angelic beings
views.case: Some ancient manuscripts, including a Dead Sea Scrolls fragment and the Septuagint, read 'sons of God' or 'angels of God,' suggesting the nations' boundaries were set with reference to a heavenly assembly, consistent with other biblical texts describing spiritual powers over nations, Daniel 10:13.

views.label: Referring to the number of Israel's sons, or Israel corporately
views.case: The Masoretic Hebrew text followed by many translations reads 'sons of Israel,' understanding the boundary of the nations as somehow calibrated to the number of Israel's own tribes or descendants, keeping the focus on Israel's election rather than a heavenly council.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that God sovereignly and purposefully ordered the nations with Israel's own place among them in view, whichever textual tradition is followed.

study.originalLanguages: God is repeatedly called tsur, Rock, verse 4, contrasted with the false 'rock' of the nations. Israel is addressed as Jeshurun, verse 15, the upright one, used ironically. And the LORD reserves naqam, vengeance, verse 35, entirely to Himself.

typology.type: The Rock who fed and sustained Israel in the wilderness

typology.fulfillment: Christ bears the wrath His people deserved, so that believers are freed to leave final justice to Him, Romans 12:19.

study.christ: 1 Corinthians 10:1-4 says Israel 'drank from the spiritual rock that followed them, and that rock was Christ,' identifying the very Rock this Song celebrates as the eternal Son who has always been present with and sustaining God's people, long before His incarnation.
study.god: God is praised here as utterly just, 'all His ways are just... righteous and upright is He,' even while His discipline of His unfaithful people is severe; yet He reserves final vengeance for Himself alone and ultimately vindicates and has compassion on His people, showing His justice and His mercy are never in conflict.

crossReferences.note: Identifies the Rock of this Song as Christ.
Quotes 'Vengeance is Mine; I will repay' to forbid personal revenge among believers.
Quotes the same verse regarding God's judgment.
Echoes the Song of Moses in the song of the redeemed before God's throne.

study.oneStory: This covenant lawsuit poem indicts Israel's coming unfaithfulness to the Rock who sustained them, reserves vengeance for God alone, and anticipates His ultimate vindication of His people, themes that find their full resolution in Christ, the Rock who followed Israel and who bears the vengeance His people deserved so that mercy might triumph.

sermon.title: What Do Christians Care About (Most)? To the Class of 2019
sermon.why: Piper builds his address directly around Deuteronomy 32:4, 'The Rock, his work is perfect, for all his ways are justice,' using this Song's central affirmation of God's justice to ground Christian concern for all suffering and injustice, a substantive engagement with this chapter's core theology of God as the just Rock.

quiz.q: What title for God dominates this Song, especially in verse 4?

options.text: The Rock.
options.note: Verse 4 introduces this title, repeated throughout the chapter, verses 15, 18, 30, 31, and 37.

options.text: The Warrior.
options.note: While God's judgment involves warfare imagery later in the chapter, the Song's dominant, opening title is the Rock.

options.text: The Shepherd.
options.note: Shepherd imagery is not the Song's central title; the Rock is.

options.text: No title is used for God in this Song.
options.note: Verse 4 gives an explicit, repeated title.

quiz.q: According to verses 10-14, how did the LORD care for Israel in the wilderness?

options.text: He found and guarded them like the apple of His eye, carried them like an eagle carries its young, and fed them abundantly.
options.note: Verses 10-14 use exactly these images.

options.text: He abandoned them to fend for themselves.
options.note: The text describes intense, attentive care, not abandonment.

options.text: He punished them constantly with no provision.
options.note: The text describes provision and care, not constant punishment, in this section.

options.text: No description of this care is given.
options.note: Verses 10-14 give a detailed, vivid description.

quiz.q: What does 'Jeshurun' do in verse 15 that provokes God?

options.text: Grows fat and kicks, abandoning the God who made him and scorning the Rock of his salvation.
options.note: Verse 15 states this explicitly.

options.text: Remains perfectly faithful throughout the wilderness.
options.note: The verse describes the opposite, rebellion after abundance.

options.text: Refuses to eat the food God provided.
options.note: The issue is ingratitude after abundance, not refusing food.

options.text: No such description is given.
options.note: Verse 15 gives this explicit, ironic description.

quiz.q: According to verse 35, to whom does vengeance belong?

options.text: To the LORD alone: 'Vengeance is Mine; I will repay.'
options.note: Verse 35 states this explicitly, quoted directly in Romans 12:19.

options.text: To whoever is strongest in battle.
options.note: The text reserves vengeance specifically to the LORD, not to human strength.

options.text: To the king of Israel.
options.note: The text reserves vengeance to the LORD, not to any human ruler.

options.text: The chapter says nothing about vengeance.
options.note: Verse 35 addresses it directly.

quiz.q: Why was Moses told he would not enter the promised land, according to verses 51-52?

options.text: Because he broke faith with the LORD at the waters of Meribah-kadesh by failing to treat Him as holy in the sight of Israel.
options.note: Verses 51-52 state this explicit reason.

options.text: Because he was too old to make the journey.
options.note: The stated reason is his specific failure at Meribah, not merely his age.

options.text: Because Joshua wanted to lead alone.
options.note: The stated reason concerns Moses' own past failure, not Joshua's preference.

options.text: No reason is given.
options.note: Verses 51-52 give an explicit reason.

questions: God is praised as the Rock, utterly stable, faithful, and just. Where in your life do you need to remember that God's character does not shift, even when your circumstances do?
Israel 'grew fat and kicked,' forgetting God in the midst of abundance. How does comfort or prosperity in your own life tend to affect your dependence on God, for better or worse?
The Song insists vengeance belongs to God alone, not to us. Where are you carrying the weight of getting even with someone, when Scripture calls you instead to release that to God?

Paul says the Rock that sustained Israel in the wilderness was Christ Himself. How does knowing Jesus was present and active even in the Old Testament change how you read stories like the exodus and wilderness wandering?

Moses, for one failure decades earlier, was kept from entering the land, yet still was allowed to see it and left an enduring legacy. Where do you need to trust God's just but merciful dealing with your own past failures?

translationNotes.note: Some ancient manuscripts, including fragments from Qumran and the Septuagint, read 'sons of God' rather than the Masoretic Hebrew's 'sons of Israel'; both readings affirm God's sovereign ordering of the nations, whether with reference to a heavenly assembly or to Israel's own number.
'Jeshurun' is a poetic, affectionate name for Israel, likely meaning 'upright one,' used here with deliberate irony as Israel grows crooked and rebellious despite the name.

'Vengeance is Mine; I will repay' is quoted directly by Paul in Romans 12:19 and by the author of Hebrews in 10:30, both times to ground the New Testament's teaching that personal revenge belongs to God alone, not to His people.
